dimensions.dimension_name: Impulse versus deliberate purchase decision-making style
dimensions.what_it_captures: The tendency to make quick, emotion-driven purchases versus slow, information-driven decisions when encountering new products or ads
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Impulsive individuals may respond positively to limited-time offers or flashy packaging without deep evaluation, while deliberate individuals require detailed information, comparisons, and reviews before considering a purchase
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Impulsive responders are more likely to click on catchy, visually appealing ads with urgent calls-to-action, while deliberate responders ignore such ads unless they provide substantive claims or expert endorsements
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: From loyalty to Dove and reliance on reviews (Q24, Q59), the person may lean deliberate, but their fun-loving personality (Q6) and mood-dependent grooming (Q30) leave uncertainty about impulse behavior in novel contexts
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (impulsive) sees a body wash ad with vibrant colors and 'limited edition' text and immediately adds to cart; Twin B (deliberate) sees the same ad, skips it, and later researches ingredients and reviews before deciding

dimensions.dimension_name: Emotional versus rational advertising engagement preference
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether storytelling, emotional appeals (e.g., joy, nostalgia) or factual, benefit-based claims drive attention and trust in advertisements
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Emotionally engaged individuals resonate with concepts that evoke feelings (e.g., self-care, confidence) and aspirational branding, while rationally engaged individuals focus on ingredient lists, clinical proofs, and practical benefits
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Emotional responders have higher engagement with video ads featuring relatable narratives or mood-enhancing visuals, while rational responders prefer text-heavy ads, ingredient breakdowns, or expert testimonials
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: Trust in honest reviews (Q56) and preference for expert breakdowns (Q58) suggest rational leanings, but the lack of questions on emotional responses leaves this dimension uncertain
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (emotional) watches a heartwarming ad about daily renewal and feels compelled to try the product; Twin B (rational) sees the same ad, dismisses it as fluff, and only engages with an ad listing pH-balanced claims

dimensions.dimension_name: Visual versus textual information processing bias
dimensions.what_it_captures: Preference for absorbing product information through visual elements (e.g., packaging design, ad visuals) versus textual content (e.g., descriptions, reviews)
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Visual processors judge product concepts heavily by packaging aesthetics and ad imagery, while textual processors prioritize written details like ingredients, benefits, and user reviews
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Visual processors are more likely to engage with video or image-based ads on social media, while textual processors respond better to blog posts, detailed captions, or review platforms
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: From preferring natural packaging (Q42) and discovering via YouTube (Q54), visual cues might matter, but no questions directly assess processing bias, leaving it ambiguous
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (visual) loves a body wash concept due to its minimalist, eco-friendly packaging design; Twin B (textual) ignores the packaging and only considers the concept after reading the ingredient list online

dimensions.what_it_captures: How current mood, occasion, or environmental context (e.g., stress, leisure time, social setting) influences openness to new products and ad engagement
dimensions.concept_test_impact: In positive moods or relaxed contexts, individuals may be more receptive to premium or innovative concepts; in negative moods or busy settings, they may default to trusted, value-oriented options
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ads seen during personal leisure time or in uplifting contexts (e.g., after exercise) yield higher click-through and purchase intent, while ads encountered during work or stress are ignored or negatively perceived
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: Mood-dependent grooming routine (Q30) hints at context sensitivity, but no questions explore how mood affects ad or concept responses, making this dimension uncaptured
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A sees a body wash ad while relaxing on weekend and considers trying it; Twin B sees the same ad during a hectic workday and scrolls past without interest

dimensions.dimension_name: Social conformity versus individuality in product choice
dimensions.what_it_captures: The degree to which peer influence, social trends, or desire to fit in drive product adoption versus personal, independent preferences
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Conformists prefer concepts that are popular or endorsed by social circles, while individualists seek unique, niche products that align with personal values, even if less mainstream
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ads leveraging social proof (e.g., 'best-seller', influencer crowds) resonate with conformists, while ads emphasizing individuality, customization, or anti-trend messaging appeal to individualists
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: Trust in friends/family (Q17) and discovery via social circles (Q54) suggest conformity, but loyalty to Dove since childhood (Q44) may indicate habit over trend-following, leaving this dimension uncertain
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (conformist) tries a new body wash after seeing multiple friends use it on social media; Twin B (individualist) avoids it, opting for a lesser-known brand that matches their specific skin needs
